hundred.en: Cold soba with spicy grated daikon
Cold soba with hot duck-and-leek dipping broth
Cold noodles with toppings and concentrated broth poured over

hundred.ja: おでん じゃがいも

hundred.en: Grilled fresh wheat-gluten dengaku with miso

tempura.history: Batter-frying arrived with 16th-century Portuguese missionaries in Nagasaki; Edo-era street stalls turned it into the light, lacy fritter we know, later refined into haute cuisine in Tokyo.
tempura.culture: A tempura specialist fries to order at the counter, piece by piece, served with tentsuyu dip or just salt; the season shows in the vegetables and seafood chosen.
tempura.gf: Standard batter is wheat flour and tentsuyu usually contains wheat soy sauce — not GF. Ask for salt instead of sauce; rice-flour batter is rare.
tempura.vegan: Vegetable (yasai) tempura can be vegan if the batter has no egg and the oil is clean — but tentsuyu often contains fish dashi.

tonkatsu.history: A Meiji-era Tokyo invention (Rengatei, 1899), adapting the French côtelette into a deep-fried panko pork loin — a landmark of yōshoku (Western-influenced Japanese food).
tonkatsu.culture: Served sliced over shredded cabbage with rice and a thick Worcester-style sauce; you grind your own sesame to mix in.
tonkatsu.gf: Panko breading and tonkatsu sauce are both wheat — not GF.

ramen.history: A Chinese wheat-noodle soup that arrived around 1900, exploded after WWII on cheap imported wheat, and became Japan's defining bowl — endlessly regionalized.
ramen.culture: Order by ticket machine, slurp (it cools and aerates the noodles), and don't linger. Broth, tare (seasoning), and aroma oil are three separate craft axes.
ramen.gf: Among the least celiac-safe dishes: wheat noodles with kansui, wheat-based tare, and often pork/fish broth. Genuinely GF (rice-noodle) ramen is very rare.
ramen.vegan: Most broth is pork or fish even when called "shoyu," but a real vegan-ramen scene (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) exists and is notably strong in Kyoto.

katsu_don.history: A Taishō-era idea layering tonkatsu over rice, simmered with onion and egg in a sweet dashi-soy broth.
katsu_don.culture: Beloved comfort food and a good-luck meal before exams — katsu also means "to win."
katsu_don.gf: Breaded cutlet plus soy-mirin broth — not GF.
katsu_don.vegan: Egg, pork, and dashi; not vegan.

udon.history: Thick wheat noodles with roots in Chinese flour culture and Buddhist temples; Sanuki (Kagawa) is the spiritual home.
udon.culture: Served hot in dashi (kake) or cold (zaru); fast and cheap, with toppings naming the bowl — kitsune (fried tofu), tanuki (tempura bits).
udon.gf: Wheat noodles and dashi-soy broth — not GF.
udon.vegan: The noodles are usually vegan, but the dashi is bonito/fish — ask for kombu dashi. Kitsune's fried tofu is plant-based.

okonomiyaki.history: Descended from Edo-era thin sweets and wartime flour cookery, the savory "grill-what-you-like" pancake matured in Osaka and Hiroshima.
okonomiyaki.culture: Often cooked at your table on a teppan; Kansai mixes batter and cabbage, Hiroshima layers in noodles — topped with okonomi sauce, mayo, bonito, and aonori.
okonomiyaki.gf: Wheat-flour batter and wheat-based sauce — not GF.
okonomiyaki.vegan: Usually has egg, dashi, pork, and bonito; needs a custom batter to be vegan.

yakitori.history: Charcoal-grilled chicken skewers that rose with Meiji-era poultry eating and post-war izakaya culture.
yakitori.culture: Eaten part-by-part — thigh, skin, liver, tsukune — with salt or tare, beer in hand; the social glue of an izakaya.
yakitori.gf: Salt (shio) skewers can be GF; the tare glaze contains wheat soy sauce — order shio.
yakitori.vegan: Chicken-based; not vegan, though grilled-vegetable skewers may be offered.

unagi.history: Freshwater eel grilled as kabayaki, prized since the Edo period and eaten on the midsummer "Day of the Ox" for stamina.
unagi.culture: Split, steamed, then lacquered with sweet soy tare over charcoal and served on rice (unadon/unajū) — a splurge.
unagi.gf: Kabayaki tare contains wheat soy sauce — not GF.

onigiri.history: Ancient portable rice carried by travelers and samurai for centuries; the konbini era made it ubiquitous.
onigiri.culture: Hand-formed rice with a filling — umeboshi, salmon, kombu — wrapped in nori; everyday fuel.
onigiri.gf: Plain-salt (shio) or umeboshi onigiri can be GF; avoid soy-marinated, fried, or seasoned fillings and check labels.
onigiri.vegan: Umeboshi, kombu, and plain-salt versions are vegan; tuna-mayo and salmon are not.

oden.history: Evolved from Muromachi-era miso-dengaku tofu into a simmered winter hotpot in a light dashi.
oden.culture: A konbini and izakaya winter staple — you choose items (daikon, egg, konnyaku, fishcakes) from a simmering pot.
oden.gf: The broth is dashi-soy (wheat) and many items are fish-paste — mostly not GF.
oden.vegan: Daikon, konnyaku, and tofu are plant-based, but the base dashi is fish — ask for kombu dashi.

yuba.history: Tofu skin, introduced with Zen Buddhism from China — a pillar of Kyoto's temple and refined cuisine.
yuba.culture: The delicate film lifted from heated soy milk, served fresh (nama-yuba) sashimi-style, in broth, or simmered. A Kyoto specialty.
yuba.gf: Yuba itself is just soybean and GF, but dipping sauces and broths may contain wheat soy sauce.
yuba.vegan: Pure soybean — vegan, and a prized plant protein.

obanzai_moriawase.history: Kyoto's traditional home cooking — thrifty, seasonal small dishes rooted in the old city's merchant and temple frugality.
obanzai_moriawase.culture: An assortment of simmered and seasoned vegetable and tofu sides; the everyday counterpoint to formal kaiseki.
obanzai_moriawase.gf: Often vegetable-based, but seasoning frequently uses soy and dashi — confirm rather than assume GF.
obanzai_moriawase.vegan: Many obanzai are plant-based, but fish dashi is common; ask which dishes are shōjin (Buddhist-vegetarian).

shojin_ryori_course.history: Buddhist temple cuisine codified by Zen monks; Kyoto, with its great temples, is its heartland — and the root of Japanese vegan cooking.
shojin_ryori_course.culture: Strictly plant-based and free of onion and garlic; an aesthetic, meditative multi-course meal celebrating tofu, yuba, and the seasons.
shojin_ryori_course.gf: Plant-based, but soy sauce (wheat) is used — request tamari or a GF option if celiac.

tofu.history: Tofu arrived with Buddhism; Kyoto's soft water made it a craft, and yudofu (simmered tofu) a temple-town specialty around Nanzen-ji.
tofu.culture: Blocks gently simmered in kombu broth and dipped in ponzu or soy with condiments — pure, quiet eating.
tofu.gf: Tofu is GF; the dipping ponzu/soy usually contains wheat — ask.
tofu.vegan: Yudofu with kombu broth is vegan (confirm there's no bonito in the dip).

nasu_dengaku.history: Dengaku — miso-glazed grilling named for a ritual dance — dates to the Muromachi era; eggplant is the classic.
nasu_dengaku.culture: Halved eggplant grilled and lacquered with sweet miso, a beloved seasonal side.
nasu_dengaku.gf: Miso can contain barley or wheat — check; otherwise simple.
nasu_dengaku.vegan: Usually vegan, though some miso glazes add dashi — confirm.

warabimochi.history: A bracken-starch jelly enjoyed since the Heian court, associated with Kansai summers.
warabimochi.culture: Soft, cool, translucent pieces dusted with kinako (roasted soy flour) and drizzled with kuromitsu syrup — a refreshing wagashi.
warabimochi.gf: True warabi-starch versions are GF, but many use wheat starch — verify.
warabimochi.vegan: Plant-based (starch, soy flour, sugar syrup) — vegan.

shoyu_ramen.history: The original Tokyo-style ramen (1910s, Asakusa's Rairaiken) — a clear chicken-and-dashi broth seasoned with a soy (shoyu) tare.
shoyu_ramen.culture: The most classic ramen profile: light brown, restrained, noodle-forward.
shoyu_ramen.gf: Wheat noodles, wheat soy tare, and usually pork/fish broth — not GF.
shoyu_ramen.vegan: Chicken/fish broth and soy tare; rarely vegan.

miso_ramen.history: Born in 1950s Sapporo (Aji no Sanpei), a hearty miso-tare ramen built for Hokkaido winters.
miso_ramen.culture: Rich and sweet-savory, often with corn, butter, and ground pork — the northern icon.
miso_ramen.gf: Wheat noodles; miso tare may contain barley/wheat; pork broth — not GF.
miso_ramen.vegan: Usually pork broth and ground pork; vegan miso ramen exists but is the exception.

chuka_soba.history: An older name for soy-based ramen — literally "Chinese noodles" — evoking the Showa shokudo era.
chuka_soba.culture: Nostalgic, simple ramen; the retro term signals a classic style.
chuka_soba.gf: Wheat noodles, soy tare, pork/fish broth — not GF.

hiyashi_chuka.history: Cold ramen with toppings and a tangy sauce — a Showa-era summer invention.
hiyashi_chuka.culture: Summer-only at many shops, with colorful julienned toppings.
hiyashi_chuka.gf: Wheat noodles + soy/vinegar sauce (wheat) — not GF.
hiyashi_chuka.vegan: Usually has ham, egg, and sometimes dashi — not vegan as standard.

champon.history: Nagasaki's hearty bowl, born from Chinese student cooking — noodles, pork, seafood, and vegetables in a milky broth.
champon.culture: Nagasaki's pride; a whole meal in a bowl.
champon.gf: Wheat noodles + pork/seafood broth — not GF.

kake.history: "Kake" is noodles served simply in hot dashi broth — the most basic preparation of udon or soba.
kake.culture: The everyday standby: cheap, fast, warming, and the canvas for toppings.
kake.gf: Broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish); udon is wheat and soba is usually wheat-cut — not GF.
kake.vegan: Bonito dashi; ask for kombu. Noodles themselves are often vegan.

kake_soba.history: Hot soba served simply in dashi broth — the most honest soba.
kake_soba.gf: Most soba is cut with wheat, plus soy-dashi broth — GF only as juwari (100% buckwheat) with a GF broth.
kake_soba.vegan: Bonito broth; a kombu version is vegan.

zaru.history: Chilled noodles on a bamboo zaru with dipping tsuyu — Edo summer eating.
zaru.culture: Refreshing and minimalist; dip, slurp, then drink the cooking water (sobayu).
zaru.gf: Tsuyu is soy + dashi (wheat + fish); soba is usually wheat-cut, udon is wheat — not GF.
zaru.vegan: Tsuyu uses bonito dashi — vegan only if kombu-based.

zaru_soba.history: The definitive cold soba on a bamboo screen, topped with nori — Edo's summer noodle.
zaru_soba.culture: Purists judge a shop by it: dip lightly and slurp.
zaru_soba.gf: Most soba is cut with wheat (nihachi); only juwari (100% buckwheat) is GF — and the tsuyu still has wheat soy + fish.
zaru_soba.vegan: Tsuyu is bonito-based; a kombu tsuyu makes it vegan.

oroshi_soba.history: Cold soba with grated daikon (oroshi) — a Fukui specialty that cuts the richness.
oroshi_soba.culture: Sharp and refreshing; the daikon adds bite.
oroshi_soba.gf: Soba usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF unless juwari + GF tsuyu.
oroshi_soba.vegan: Bonito tsuyu; a kombu version is vegan.

tororo_soba.history: Soba topped with grated yam (tororo) — a slippery, nourishing classic.
tororo_soba.culture: Prized as a stamina dish for its silky texture.
tororo_soba.gf: Soba is usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF unless juwari; the yam itself is GF.
tororo_soba.vegan: Bonito tsuyu; the yam is vegan — ask for kombu tsuyu.

kamo_seiro.history: Cold soba dipped in a hot duck-and-leek broth — a refined Edo combination.
kamo_seiro.culture: A connoisseur's favorite, especially in cold months.
kamo_seiro.gf: Soba usually wheat-cut + soy-dashi duck broth — not GF.

sobagaki.history: Buckwheat "dumpling" — soba flour kneaded with hot water into a soft mass, an old rustic dish predating cut noodles.
sobagaki.culture: A purist's way to taste the buckwheat itself, dipped in soy or eaten with wasabi.
sobagaki.gf: If 100% buckwheat the gaki is GF — but the dipping soy is wheat; confirm no wheat in the flour.
sobagaki.vegan: Buckwheat and water — vegan; watch the dip.

kitsune.history: Noodles topped with sweet fried tofu (aburaage) — named for the fox's mythical love of it.
kitsune.culture: A gentle, beloved bowl; a Kansai signature comfort food.
kitsune.gf: Broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish) and the noodles are wheat — not GF.
kitsune.vegan: The aburaage is plant-based, but the broth is fish dashi — ask for kombu.

kitsune_udon.history: Udon topped with sweet fried tofu (aburaage) — the Kansai comfort bowl par excellence (Osaka origin).
kitsune_udon.culture: Gentle and sweet-savory; the everyday favorite.
kitsune_udon.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
kitsune_udon.vegan: Aburaage is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask for kombu.

tanuki.history: Noodles topped with crispy tempura-batter bits (tenkasu) — "tanuki" for the trickster with no real tempura.
tanuki.culture: A cheap, satisfying favorite; in Osaka "tanuki" instead means kitsune soba.
tanuki.gf: Tenkasu is fried wheat batter + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
tanuki.vegan: Broth is fish dashi; tenkasu may be vegan — ask about the broth.

niku_udon.history: Udon topped with sweet-simmered beef (or pork) — hearty everyday fare.
niku_udon.culture: Filling and warming; popular in western Japan.
niku_udon.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi broth + soy-simmered meat — not GF.
niku_udon.vegan: Meat and fish dashi — not vegan.

tempura_udon.history: Udon in hot dashi topped with tempura, often shrimp — comfort and crunch together.
tempura_udon.culture: A hearty classic; the tempura softens in the broth.
tempura_udon.gf: Wheat noodles + wheat batter + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
tempura_udon.vegan: Fish dashi and usually shrimp — not vegan.

nabeyaki_udon.history: Udon simmered in an individual clay pot with tempura, egg, and vegetables — a winter classic.
nabeyaki_udon.culture: Served bubbling in its donabe; a meal in a pot.
nabeyaki_udon.gf: Wheat udon + tempura + soy-dashi broth — not GF.
nabeyaki_udon.vegan: Egg, fish dashi, often shrimp tempura — not vegan.

bukkake.history: Noodles with a little concentrated tsuyu poured directly over (not for dipping) — a Sanuki/Okayama style.
bukkake.culture: Bold, direct flavor, topped with daikon, scallion, and tempura bits.
bukkake.gf: Wheat udon + soy-dashi tsuyu — not GF.
bukkake.vegan: Bonito tsuyu and varied toppings — ask for kombu tsuyu.

ebi_tempura.history: Shrimp tempura — the star of the tempura world since Edo.
ebi_tempura.culture: The benchmark piece: straight, golden, crisp.
ebi_tempura.gf: Wheat batter; tentsuyu has wheat soy — not GF.

tendon.history: A Meiji-era donburi piling tempura over rice with a sweet soy tare.
tendon.culture: Hearty tempura turned into a full meal.
tendon.gf: Wheat batter + wheat-soy tare — not GF.
tendon.vegan: Tare/dashi and usually shrimp; vegetable tendon needs egg-free batter and vegan tare.

tempura_moriawase.history: An assortment of tempura — the chef's selection of seafood and vegetables.
tempura_moriawase.culture: A shareable spread showcasing the season.
tempura_moriawase.vegan: Usually includes seafood; vegetable-only with egg-free batter could be vegan — ask.

tempura_set.history: A tempura teishoku — assorted tempura with rice, miso soup, and pickles.
tempura_set.gf: Wheat batter, wheat tentsuyu, and miso soup (dashi) — not GF.
tempura_set.vegan: Usually seafood + fish dashi — not vegan.

tonkatsu_teishoku.history: The tonkatsu set meal — cutlet with rice, miso soup, cabbage, and pickles — the standard teishoku format.
tonkatsu_teishoku.culture: A complete, balanced lunch; teishoku is the backbone of everyday dining.
tonkatsu_teishoku.gf: Breaded cutlet, wheat sauce, and miso soup (fish dashi) — not GF.
tonkatsu_teishoku.vegan: Pork, egg, and dashi — not vegan.

katsu_sando.history: A breaded-cutlet sandwich on soft shokupan — a Showa-era yoshoku creation now turned gourmet.
katsu_sando.culture: Both a konbini staple and a fancy-café indulgence.
katsu_sando.gf: Wheat bread + panko cutlet + wheat sauce — not GF.
katsu_sando.vegan: Pork, egg, and often butter/mayo — not vegan.

kushikatsu.history: Skewered, panko-breaded, deep-fried morsels — Osaka's Shinsekai working-class icon (never double-dip the communal sauce!).
kushikatsu.culture: Cheap, social, and addictive; meat, seafood, and vegetables all skewered.
kushikatsu.gf: Panko breading + wheat-based dipping sauce — not GF.
kushikatsu.vegan: Often meat/seafood, and the batter usually has egg/wheat — not assured vegan.

gyudon.history: Thin beef and onion simmered in sweet soy-dashi over rice — Meiji-era fast food (Yoshinoya, 1899).
gyudon.culture: Cheap, quick, ubiquitous — the salaryman's standby.

oyako_don.history: "Parent-and-child" bowl of chicken and egg simmered over rice — a Meiji-era Tokyo invention (Tamahide).
oyako_don.vegan: Chicken, egg, and dashi — not vegan.

konoha_don.history: A "fallen-leaf" donburi of egg-bound kamaboko (fish cake) over rice — a thrifty regional bowl.
konoha_don.culture: A lighter cousin of katsudon and oyakodon.
konoha_don.gf: Egg + fish cake (wheat) + soy-dashi — not GF.
konoha_don.vegan: Egg and fish cake/dashi — not vegan.

tamago_toji.history: Ingredients bound with beaten egg in a dashi-soy broth ("egg-curtained") — the technique behind oyakodon and katsudon.
tamago_toji.culture: Soft and comforting; a home-cooking staple.

takikomi_gohan.history: Rice cooked together with seasonal ingredients in dashi and soy — a centuries-old way to stretch rice and mark the season.
takikomi_gohan.culture: Autumn's mushroom and chestnut versions are especially loved.
takikomi_gohan.gf: Seasoned with soy (wheat) and dashi (fish) — usually not GF.
takikomi_gohan.vegan: Often fish dashi and sometimes chicken; a vegetable + kombu version can be vegan.

ochazuke.history: Rice with tea or dashi poured over — an old, thrifty way to finish a meal, refined in the Edo period.
ochazuke.culture: Comfort in a bowl; toppings like umeboshi, salmon, or nori; a gentle end to a night out.
ochazuke.gf: Broth/tea may carry soy or dashi (wheat/fish); plain green-tea ochazuke with salt is closer to GF — verify toppings.
ochazuke.vegan: Umeboshi/nori versions can be vegan, but many use dashi or fish toppings.

zosui.history: A rice soup simmered in dashi, often from leftover nabe broth — old, frugal, and restorative.
zosui.culture: The comforting finish to a hotpot, or a sickbed food.
zosui.gf: Dashi + soy (wheat + fish) — usually not GF.
zosui.vegan: Usually fish dashi and often egg/meat; a vegetable + kombu version can be vegan.

bento.history: Portable boxed meals dating back centuries; the ekiben (station bento) is a travel ritual.
bento.culture: A balanced, compartmentalized meal — both an art form and everyday convenience.
bento.gf: Mixed contents typically include soy-seasoned and fried (wheat) items — assume not GF without checking each.
bento.vegan: Usually contains fish, meat, or egg; vegan bento exist but are uncommon.

shojin_bento.history: A boxed form of Buddhist temple cuisine — plant-based, seasonal, balanced.
shojin_bento.gf: Plant-based, but soy sauce (wheat) is common — ask for tamari/GF.

shojin_ryori_set.history: A set-meal form of Buddhist temple cuisine — plant-based, seasonal, balanced.
shojin_ryori_set.culture: A more casual entry point to shojin than the full course.
shojin_ryori_set.gf: Plant-based but soy sauce (wheat) is used — ask for tamari/GF.

kaiseki_course.history: The pinnacle of Japanese haute cuisine, evolved from tea-ceremony meals — a seasonal multi-course progression.
kaiseki_course.culture: Aesthetic, seasonal, and exquisitely sequenced; the height of washoku.
kaiseki_course.gf: Many courses use soy and dashi (wheat + fish); a good ryotei can adapt with advance notice — flag celiac ahead.
kaiseki_course.vegan: Built around fish and dashi — request a shojin (vegan) kaiseki in advance.

omakase_course.history: "I leave it to you" — the chef-curated tasting, especially at sushi counters; a modern refinement of trust-based dining.
omakase_course.culture: The pinnacle of a specialist meal; sequence and timing are the chef's art.
omakase_course.gf: Will include soy sauce and likely wheat-containing items — tell the chef about celiac ahead (some adapt with tamari).
omakase_course.vegan: Seafood/dashi-centered — not vegan unless a vegan omakase is arranged in advance.

nimono.history: Simmered dishes — vegetables, tofu, fish, or meat cooked slowly in dashi, soy, mirin, and sugar — a cornerstone of home and obanzai cooking.
nimono.culture: The quiet heart of washoku; seasonal and comforting.
nimono.gf: Simmering liquid uses soy (wheat) and dashi — usually not GF; ask.
nimono.vegan: Often fish dashi; a vegetable nimono with kombu dashi can be vegan — confirm.

takiawase.history: Vegetables, tofu, and sometimes fish simmered separately then arranged together — a refined obanzai/kaiseki technique.
takiawase.culture: Celebrates each ingredient's own seasoning and the season.
takiawase.gf: Simmered in soy + dashi (wheat + fish) — usually not GF; ask.
takiawase.vegan: Often fish dashi; vegetable + kombu versions can be vegan — confirm.

dashimaki_tamago.history: A rolled omelette bound with dashi, refined in Kyoto/Kansai where it's softer and less sweet than Tokyo's tamagoyaki.
dashimaki_tamago.culture: A measure of a chef's skill; served at sushi counters and as obanzai.
dashimaki_tamago.gf: Egg + dashi + a little soy/mirin — dashi/soy may contain wheat, and it usually has fish dashi.
dashimaki_tamago.vegan: Egg-based with fish dashi — not vegan.

chawanmushi.history: A savory steamed egg custard in dashi with hidden morsels — refined Edo-era kaiseki cooking.
chawanmushi.culture: Silky and delicate; a beloved hot starter.
chawanmushi.gf: Egg + dashi + soy/mirin — dashi/soy may contain wheat, usually fish dashi.
chawanmushi.vegan: Egg + fish dashi (often with chicken/shrimp inside) — not vegan.

agedashi_tofu.history: Lightly fried tofu in a dashi broth — a classic izakaya dish with temple-cuisine roots.
agedashi_tofu.culture: Silky inside, crisp outside; gentle and warming.
agedashi_tofu.gf: Tofu is dusted with potato or wheat starch (varies) and served in dashi-soy broth — confirm; often not GF.
agedashi_tofu.vegan: Usually fish dashi; can be made vegan with kombu dashi — ask.

agedashi_dofu.history: Lightly fried tofu in a dashi broth — a classic izakaya dish with temple-cuisine roots (same as agedashi-tofu).
agedashi_dofu.culture: Silky inside, crisp outside; gentle and warming.
agedashi_dofu.gf: Tofu dusted with potato or wheat starch + dashi-soy broth — confirm; often not GF.
agedashi_dofu.vegan: Usually fish dashi; vegan with kombu dashi — ask.

ganmodoki.history: A deep-fried tofu fritter studded with vegetables — Buddhist-cuisine origins; the name nods to "mock goose."
ganmodoki.culture: Simmered in oden or nimono; hearty and savory.
ganmodoki.gf: May use a yam/flour binder and is simmered in soy-dashi — confirm; not assumed GF.
ganmodoki.vegan: Traditionally vegan (tofu + vegetables), but some add egg and it's often simmered in fish dashi — ask.

fu_dengaku.history: Wheat-gluten (fu) glazed with sweet miso and grilled — a temple-cuisine and Kyoto specialty (nama-fu is a Kyoto craft).
fu_dengaku.gf: Fu is pure wheat gluten — emphatically NOT GF.
fu_dengaku.vegan: Fu and miso glaze are plant-based — usually vegan (confirm the miso has no dashi).

saikyo_yaki.history: Fish marinated in sweet Kyoto Saikyo white miso, then grilled — a refined Kyoto preparation.
saikyo_yaki.culture: Elegant and sweet-savory; classic kaiseki and obanzai.
saikyo_yaki.gf: Saikyo miso may contain wheat/barley — confirm; the marinade is the risk.
saikyo_yaki.vegan: Fish (typically cod or sawara) — not vegan.

buta_kakuni.history: Pork belly braised until meltingly tender in soy, sugar, and sake — Nagasaki/Kyushu roots (from Chinese dongpo pork).
buta_kakuni.culture: Rich and indulgent; falls apart at a touch.
buta_kakuni.gf: Braised in soy sauce (wheat) — not GF.

tonjiru.history: A hearty miso soup with pork and root vegetables — warming farmhouse cooking.
tonjiru.culture: A filling side that's almost a meal; winter comfort.
tonjiru.vegan: Pork and fish dashi — not vegan.

tsukune.history: Minced chicken meatballs grilled on skewers (yakitori) or simmered, sometimes with a raw egg-yolk dip.
tsukune.culture: A yakitori favorite — juicy, often glazed with tare.
tsukune.gf: Tare has wheat soy and binders may include flour — usually not GF; salt versions are safer.

negiyaki.history: A thinner, green-onion-heavy cousin of okonomiyaki from Osaka.
negiyaki.culture: Lighter and more savory; griddle comfort food.
negiyaki.vegan: Usually egg, dashi, and often meat — not vegan as standard.

anpan.history: A sweet roll filled with red-bean paste, invented in 1874 (Kimuraya, Tokyo) — the birth of Japanese pastry (kashi-pan).
anpan.culture: An everyday bakery and konbini staple; nostalgic and beloved.
anpan.vegan: The dough usually has egg/dairy — typically not vegan, though the anko filling is plant-based.

zenzai.history: A warm sweet red-bean soup with mochi — an old comfort sweet, especially in winter; Kansai's name for it.
zenzai.culture: Cozy and nostalgic; served at tea houses and festivals.
zenzai.gf: Azuki and sugar are GF, and mochi is glutinous RICE (GF) — confirm no wheat additives.

yokan.history: A firm jelled sweet of red-bean paste and agar — a centuries-old wagashi, originally a Chinese import.
yokan.culture: Dense and refined; sliced and served with tea, and a classic gift.

wagashi.history: Traditional Japanese confections, refined alongside the tea ceremony over centuries.
wagashi.culture: Seasonal, beautiful, and restrained in sweetness — the companion to matcha.
wagashi.gf: Varies widely: rice/mochi-based ones can be GF, but many (manju, dorayaki) use wheat — always check.
wagashi.vegan: Often plant-based (beans, rice, sugar, agar), but some use egg (e.g. castella) — check.

sashimi.history: Sliced raw fish — Japan's purest expression of seafood, refined over centuries.
sashimi.culture: Eaten early in a meal; the knife work and freshness are everything, dipped in soy with wasabi.
sashimi.gf: The fish is naturally GF, but soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

maguro_sashimi.history: Sliced raw tuna — the most iconic sashimi.
maguro_sashimi.culture: From lean akami to fatty toro; the measure of a fish counter.
maguro_sashimi.gf: Tuna is GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

sashimi_moriawase.history: An assorted sashimi platter — the chef's selection of the day's best fish.
sashimi_moriawase.culture: A centerpiece for sharing, arranged with seasonal garnish.
sashimi_moriawase.gf: Fish is GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

sushi_nigiri.history: Hand-pressed vinegared rice topped with fish — Edo's 19th-century fast food, now global.
sushi_nigiri.culture: The canonical sushi form, eaten in one bite, fish-side to the tongue.
sushi_nigiri.gf: Rice and fish are GF, but soy sauce is wheat and some toppings (eel, imitation crab) contain wheat — use tamari, choose plain fish.
sushi_nigiri.vegan: Fish-topped; not vegan (except tamago = egg, kappa, inari).

maguro_nigiri.history: Tuna over vinegared rice — the king of Edomae nigiri; once disdained, now the most prized.
maguro_nigiri.culture: The sushi benchmark, from lean akami to fatty toro.
maguro_nigiri.gf: Fish and rice are GF, but soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

otoro_nigiri.history: The fattiest tuna belly — the most luxurious, expensive nigiri.
otoro_nigiri.culture: Melts on the tongue; the splurge piece.
otoro_nigiri.gf: Tuna and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

salmon_nigiri.history: A modern arrival — Norway introduced raw salmon to Japan in the 1980s; now hugely popular.
salmon_nigiri.culture: A crowd-pleaser, especially with younger diners and visitors.
salmon_nigiri.gf: Fish and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

hamachi_nigiri.history: Yellowtail nigiri — rich and buttery, a winter favorite (especially mature buri).
hamachi_nigiri.culture: Beloved for its fattiness; a sushi-bar staple.
hamachi_nigiri.gf: Fish and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

madai_nigiri.history: Sea bream (tai) nigiri — a celebratory white fish, auspicious in Japan.
madai_nigiri.culture: Prized for clean flavor; served at celebrations.
madai_nigiri.gf: Fish and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

ebi_nigiri.history: Cooked shrimp nigiri — a classic, approachable piece.
ebi_nigiri.culture: Often the gateway nigiri for the raw-averse.
ebi_nigiri.gf: Shrimp and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

ama_ebi_nigiri.history: Sweet raw shrimp (amaebi) nigiri — delicate and prized.
ama_ebi_nigiri.culture: Valued for its silky sweetness, sometimes topped with its roe.
ama_ebi_nigiri.gf: Shrimp and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

ika_nigiri.history: Squid nigiri — tender and subtly sweet, sometimes scored for texture.
ika_nigiri.gf: Squid and rice are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

tamago_nigiri.history: Sweet egg omelette over rice — traditionally the piece that tests a sushi chef, often eaten last.
tamago_nigiri.culture: A gentle, sweet finish to a sushi meal.
tamago_nigiri.gf: Egg and rice, but tamago usually contains dashi and sometimes a little flour/mirin — confirm.

gunkan_maki.history: "Battleship" sushi — a rice oval wrapped in nori to hold loose toppings, invented at Ginza Kyubey in 1941.
gunkan_maki.culture: The vessel for roe, uni, and soft toppings.
gunkan_maki.gf: Rice and nori are GF, but toppings and soy sauce vary (wheat) — use tamari, check toppings.

ikura_gunkan.history: Salmon-roe battleship — glistening orange pearls in a nori cup.
ikura_gunkan.culture: A prized, briny burst; visually iconic.
ikura_gunkan.gf: Rice, nori, and roe are GF, but cured roe may contain soy/wheat — check; soy sauce is wheat (use tamari).

negitoro_gunkan.history: Minced fatty tuna with green onion in a gunkan — a modern favorite.
negitoro_gunkan.culture: Rich and savory; an affordable luxury.
negitoro_gunkan.gf: Rice/nori/tuna are GF, but negitoro prep may include soy — check; soy sauce is wheat (use tamari).

tekka_maki.history: A tuna roll said to be named for the "tekka" gambling halls where it was a quick bite.
tekka_maki.culture: A clean, classic maki — a sushi-counter standard.
tekka_maki.gf: Rice, tuna, and nori are GF, but soy sauce is wheat — use tamari and check the rice vinegar.

kappa_maki.history: Cucumber roll, named for the water sprite kappa's love of cucumber.
kappa_maki.gf: Rice, cucumber, and nori are GF; soy sauce is wheat — use tamari.

makizushi.history: Rolled sushi (norimaki) — rice and fillings rolled in nori, an Edo-era form.
makizushi.culture: From slim hosomaki to fat futomaki; everyday and festive (ehomaki at Setsubun).
makizushi.gf: Rice and nori are GF, but fillings vary (tempura, imitation crab = wheat) and soy is wheat — choose simple fillings + tamari.
makizushi.vegan: Depends on the filling — kappa/vegetable maki are vegan; fish/egg are not.

oshizushi.history: Pressed box sushi from Osaka — the older Kansai style, predating Edo's hand-pressed nigiri.
oshizushi.culture: Layered and cut into neat blocks; battera (mackerel) is iconic.
oshizushi.gf: Rice and fish are GF, but cured toppings/sauces may contain soy/wheat — check; use tamari.

inari_zushi.history: Sweet fried-tofu pouches stuffed with sushi rice — named for the Inari shrine fox.
inari_zushi.culture: Homey, sweet, and portable; a picnic and festival favorite.
inari_zushi.gf: The aburaage is simmered in soy-mirin-dashi (wheat + fish) — usually not GF despite seeming simple.
inari_zushi.vegan: Pouch and rice are plant-based, but the simmering dashi is often fish — ask; many are vegan.

sushi_moriawase.history: An assorted sushi platter — a selection of nigiri and maki.
sushi_moriawase.culture: The shareable centerpiece; the range of the day's fish.
sushi_moriawase.gf: Rice and fish are GF, but soy is wheat and some pieces (eel, imitation crab, tempura roll) contain wheat — use tamari, choose plain.
sushi_moriawase.vegan: Mostly fish — not vegan (pick the vegetable pieces).

oden_daikon.history: Simmered daikon — the most beloved oden item, soaking up the dashi over hours.
oden_daikon.culture: The first thing many order from the oden pot.
oden_daikon.gf: Daikon is plain, but the oden broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish) — not GF.
oden_daikon.vegan: Daikon is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask.

oden_tofu.history: Tofu (often grilled yaki-dofu) simmered in the oden pot.
oden_tofu.gf: Tofu is GF but the oden broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish) — not GF.
oden_tofu.vegan: Tofu is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask for kombu.

oden_jagaimo.history: Whole potato simmered in the oden pot, soaking up dashi.
oden_jagaimo.gf: Potato is plain but the oden broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish) — not GF.
oden_jagaimo.vegan: Potato is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask.

oden_konnyaku.history: Konnyaku (konjac) — a chewy, near-zero-calorie oden staple, often knotted or triangular.
oden_konnyaku.culture: Prized for texture; a classic oden item.
oden_konnyaku.gf: Konnyaku is GF, but the oden broth is dashi-soy (wheat + fish) — not GF.
oden_konnyaku.vegan: Konnyaku is plant-based; the broth is fish dashi — ask.

oden_chikuwa.history: Tube-shaped grilled fish-paste cake — a quintessential oden item.
oden_chikuwa.culture: Springy and savory; soaks up broth through its hollow.
oden_chikuwa.gf: Fish-paste (chikuwa) usually contains wheat starch, plus the dashi broth — not GF.

yaki_onigiri.history: Grilled rice balls brushed with soy or miso — old home/campfire cooking, now an izakaya staple.
yaki_onigiri.culture: Crispy, savory, and smoky; great with beer.
yaki_onigiri.gf: Brushed with soy (wheat) or miso (barley) — usually not GF; plain salt-grilled can be.
yaki_onigiri.vegan: Often vegan (rice + soy/miso), but some are brushed with dashi-soy — check.
